Local Shuswap

Cottonwood Pollen and Your Windows: A BC Shuswap Guide

Published 2026-05-27 · Wagon Windows

Every spring, the Shuswap goes white. The cottonwood and black poplar trees that line the rivers, lakeshore, and residential areas across Salmon Arm, Chase, and Sorrento shed their seeds in a blizzard of white cotton that coats everything outside. For homeowners, this means screens packed solid, tracks filled with fibre, and a film on glass that bonds quickly and requires more than a rinse to remove. Here's what cottonwood does to your windows — and the precise timing that makes the difference between an easy clean and a difficult one.

Need professional window cleaning in the Shuswap?

Book Online — Free Quote (403) 837-5092

Cottonwood Season in BC: When and Where

Cottonwood (Populus trichocarpa) and black poplar (Populus nigra) are native and widely planted throughout interior BC. They are among the most prolific seed-shedding trees in the province. In the Shuswap, shedding typically begins in the last week of May and peaks in the second and third week of June — though this varies by 1-2 weeks depending on spring temperature. Warm springs advance shedding; cool, wet springs delay it. Shuswap communities most affected by cottonwood include Salmon Arm (particularly the lower townsite, Salmon River corridor, and lakeshore areas), Chase Creek valley, Sorrento (shoreline areas adjacent to Shuswap Lake), and Blind Bay. The cottonwood density along the Salmon River from Canoe to the delta is among the highest in the region. Properties within 300 metres of major cottonwood stands receive several times more cotton deposit than hillside or escarpment properties above the main valley. In the Thompson-Okanagan broadly, Chase and Kamloops tend to peak slightly earlier than Salmon Arm due to lower elevation and warmer spring temperatures. Sicamous and the higher-elevation communities to the north typically shed 1-2 weeks later than Salmon Arm. This sequential shedding matters for service timing — Wagon Windows schedules accordingly.

  • Peak shedding: late May to late June in the Shuswap (second-third week of June most common)
  • Most affected communities: Salmon Arm lower townsite, Chase Creek, Sorrento shoreline, Blind Bay
  • Shedding advances in warm springs, delays in cool wet springs
  • Properties within 300m of major cottonwood stands receive significantly more cotton

What Cottonwood Actually Does to Windows and Screens

The fluffy white material is not the only product of cottonwood shedding — it's the most visible part. Understanding all three components helps explain why cottonwood creates a window problem that regular cleaning doesn't fully address. The cotton (seed fibre): The white fibrous material packs mechanically into window screens, door screens, and window tracks. A screen facing a cottonwood tree can reach 30-50% blockage within a single week of peak shedding. This restricts airflow through open windows and, when wet by rain or morning dew, creates a compressed, dense mat that adheres to the screen mesh. Removing packed cotton from screens requires physical removal — a hose-off or manual brush — before standard cleaning. The pollen (microscopic): Cottonwood releases true pollen 4-6 weeks before the cotton shedding, typically in April. This pollen is one of the most potent seasonal allergens in BC and deposits a fine yellow-brown layer on glass during the pollen phase. Most homeowners don't notice this phase as visibly as the cotton phase, but it leaves a thin film on glass that, when combined with the subsequent cotton-phase residue, creates a layered deposit. The sap and terpene film: Less visible but most problematic for glass. Cottonwood buds release a sticky aromatic resin in spring — the source of the distinctive sweet smell. This resin, when distributed by wind and dew cycles, deposits a very thin but chemically active film on glass and painted surfaces. This film acts as a bonding agent for subsequent pollen and dust particles, and it cures over time from a tacky surface to a more permanent coating. Standard detergent cleaning removes recent deposition; cured cottonwood resin requires a degreasing step or citrus-based solvent.

  • Cotton fibre: packs screens mechanically — physical removal required before cleaning
  • Pollen (April phase): fine film on glass, precursor to the more visible cotton phase
  • Sap/terpene film: sticky resin that bonds subsequent deposits — cures if left >2 weeks
  • Combined effect: a three-layer deposit that's more than simple dust

Why Timing Your Post-Cottonwood Clean Matters

The single most important decision in cottonwood window management is when to clean. Clean too early — during active shedding — and you've wasted the job. Clean too late — three weeks after peak — and the terpene film has cured to the point where a standard clean won't fully clear it. The target window is the first 7-14 days after shedding has fully stopped. In Salmon Arm, this typically means booking for the last week of June through the first week of July. This timing captures the terpene film while it's still in the tacky-but-soluble state, removes all cotton and pollen residue, and clears screens before the heat of summer hardens debris into the mesh. How to tell when shedding has stopped: walk outside and look at your vehicle or any horizontal surface that has been outside for 24 hours without rain. If no new cotton is accumulating, shedding is complete or nearly complete. Don't wait for zero-cotton days — the tail of the shedding season is sparse, and waiting for absolute zero means losing your optimal treatment window. If you missed the optimal window and it's been more than 3 weeks since shedding stopped, tell your window cleaner. Cured cottonwood film benefits from a pre-treatment soak with a light citrus degreaser before the standard clean. Doing a standard clean first and expecting the film to clear is one of the more common post-cottonwood service errors.

For rental properties or seasonal properties left unmonitored during cottonwood season, a late-June booking as a standing annual appointment is the safest default — regardless of monitoring. It captures the optimal window 80% of years.

DIY vs Professional: The Cottonwood Case

Cottonwood window cleaning is one of the situations where the DIY vs professional question has a clearer answer than usual. The cotton removal from screens and tracks is physically straightforward and well within DIY capability. A hose-off plus a stiff brush on screens, and a vacuum plus damp cloth on tracks, addresses the physical debris component effectively. What DIY consistently struggles with is the glass film. The pollen-plus-terpene film on glass requires the right chemistry applied with proper squeegee technique to clear without leaving residue. Using a household glass cleaner (typically alcohol-based) on a cottonwood-filmed window will often distribute the terpene film rather than removing it — leaving a smeared haze rather than clarity. This is the most common homeowner complaint: 'I cleaned my windows but they still look dirty.' Professional cleaning with a biodegradable detergent formulated for organic soil removal, applied with a saturating scrubber and cleared with a clean rubber squeegee in proper technique, will clear the cottonwood film in a single pass. For most Shuswap homeowners, the post-cottonwood clean is a once-a-year professional service call that saves 3-4 hours of DIY frustration and delivers a reliably clear result.

  • DIY handles well: screen cotton removal (hose + brush), track vacuum and wipe
  • DIY struggles with: glass film — wrong chemistry smears rather than removes terpene residue
  • Professional advantage: correct detergent chemistry + squeegee technique clears film in one pass
  • Timing matters more than who does it — book within 2 weeks of shedding end regardless

Frequently Asked Questions

When is cottonwood pollen season over in Salmon Arm?

Peak shedding in Salmon Arm typically ends between June 15-25, though warm springs can advance this to early June. The visible cotton (fluffy white seeds) is the last phase of shedding. Once you see two consecutive dry days with no new cotton accumulating on outdoor surfaces, the season is functionally over and you're in the optimal cleaning window.

Will cottonwood pollen damage my windows if left too long?

The terpene film from cottonwood doesn't etch glass the way mineral deposits do — it won't cause permanent damage. But it will cure over several weeks into a more resistant coating that requires more aggressive chemistry to remove. On windows that are also receiving mineral spray (lakefront properties), the terpene film accelerates mineral bonding by providing an adhesion surface. In that combination, delayed cleaning has compounding effects.

Do screens need to be removed to clean them after cottonwood season?

Not necessarily. Moderate cotton accumulation on screens can be cleared with a firm hose-off at angle, followed by a soft brush scrub, with screens in place. Heavy accumulation — full blockage of the mesh — is better addressed with screens removed, soaked and brushed. If your screens haven't been removed and cleaned in 2+ years, cottonwood season is a good prompt to do so.

Does Wagon Windows clean during cottonwood season?

Yes — we accept bookings during active shedding for track, screen, and debris clearing, but we advise clients that glass cleaning scheduled during peak cotton shed should be understood as a preliminary clean that will need re-doing after shedding stops. We recommend post-shedding bookings for the glass clean that counts. Call (403) 837-5092 to discuss timing for your property.
